chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction

cards.front: What is the difference between implicit and explicit type conversion?
cards.back: Implicit type conversion is performed automatically by the compiler when needed. Explicit type conversion is requested by the programmer using a cast operator like static_cast.

cards.front: What is the key difference between numeric promotions and numeric conversions?
cards.back: Numeric promotions are always safe and value-preserving (no data loss), converting smaller types to larger ones. Numeric conversions may result in data loss and are not always safe.

cards.text: {{c1::Brace initialization}} disallows {{c2::narrowing conversions}}, making it safer than copy initialization.

cards.front: Why is static_cast preferred over C-style casts?
cards.back: static_cast is safer and more visible in code. It's easier to search for, more restrictive (won't do dangerous conversions), and the compiler can verify the cast is valid.

cards.front: Do type aliases create new distinct types?
cards.back: No. Type aliases are just alternative names (synonyms) for existing types. They don't create new types and are fully interchangeable with the original type.

cards.text: Modern C++ prefers {{c1::using}} over {{c2::typedef}} for creating type aliases because of better syntax and template support.

cards.front: What does auto drop when deducing types, and how do you preserve them?
cards.back:
auto drops const and reference qualifiers by default. To preserve them:
  • Use const auto for const
  • Use auto& for references
  • Use const auto& for const references

cards.front: What do the usual arithmetic conversions ensure?
cards.back: They ensure that both operands of a binary operator are converted to the same type before the operation is performed, following a type hierarchy (long double > double > float > promoted integral types).

cards.concept: Key takeaways for type conversions and type safety
cards.explanation:
Important principles from Chapter 10:
  • Prefer brace initialization to prevent narrowing conversions
  • Use static_cast for explicit conversions, not C-style casts
  • Avoid mixing signed and unsigned types in operations
  • Be aware that narrowing conversions can lose data
  • Use auto when type is obvious, explicit types for clarity
  • Type aliases improve readability but don't add type safety
examples: int x{ 3.5 };           // Error: good, prevents data loss
auto d = 5.0 / 3;      // OK: type clear from context
using ID = int;        // Documents purpose, easier to change

cards.front: What are the 5 main categories of standard conversions?
cards.back:
• Numeric promotions (safe, value-preserving)
  • Numeric conversions (may lose data)
  • Qualification conversions (add/remove const/volatile)
  • Value transformations (lvalue to rvalue, etc.)
  • Pointer conversions

cards.text: {{c1::Implicit}} type conversion is automatic, while {{c2::explicit}} type conversion uses a cast operator.
Numeric {{c1::promotions}} are always safe and value-preserving, while numeric {{c2::conversions}} may lose data.
When using auto for type deduction, you {{c1::must}} provide an initializer because the compiler needs it to deduce the type.
For explicit type conversion, use {{c1::static_cast}} instead of {{c2::C-style casts}} for better safety and visibility.
